complete_examples.overview: Complete workflow examples for the UI Designer mode, demonstrating the code-first approach: analyze code → apply visual changes → run tests → present result. Screenshots are only requested as a fallback when the user rejects the initial result and their verbal description is insufficient.

example.name: web_app_spacing_fix
example.scenario: A user reports that the dashboard cards have inconsistent spacing — some are too close together, others have too much gap. Functionality is confirmed correct.
example.user_request: "The dashboard cards don't look right — the spacing between them is all over the place. The data is correct though, just the layout needs fixing."

step.description: Detect project type and identify target files
step.approach: Detect project type: package.json shows React + Tailwind → web app. Search for dashboard component files. Find Dashboard.tsx with card grid layout using Tailwind utility classes with inconsistent gap utilities.
step.expected_outcome: Identified target: Dashboard.tsx with inconsistent gap-* and p-* utilities. This is purely visual — spacing classes (gap-*, p-*, m-*) are safe to modify.

step.approach: Normalize the grid gap to a consistent value: gap-6 on the grid container. Standardize card padding to p-6. Remove ad-hoc margin utilities on individual cards. These are all Tailwind utility class changes — no JavaScript touched.

Run `npm test` — all 24 tests pass. Summarize changes: - Standardized grid gap to 1.5rem across all dashboard cards - Normalized card padding to 1.5rem - Removed ad-hoc margin overrides on individual cards Ask user: "Approved ✅ / Needs adjustments / Revert".
User selects "Approved." Task complete with no screenshots needed.

example.completion: Dashboard card spacing standardized using consistent Tailwind gap and padding utilities. All 24 tests pass. No JavaScript or component logic was modified. Zero screenshots needed.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The happy path uses zero screenshots — code analysis is sufficient for spacing, color, and layout fixes
Tailwind utility class changes are safely visual when limited to spacing, color, and typography utilities

example.name: user_rejects_and_provides_screenshot
example.scenario: A user asks to fix the signup form's appearance. After the first code-only attempt, the user says "it still doesn't look right" without being specific. The UI Designer offers screenshot assistance and the user provides one.
example.user_request: "The signup form looks ugly. Can you fix it?"

step.description: First attempt: code-only analysis and changes
step.approach: Detect project type: React with styled-components → web app. Read SignupForm.tsx. The form has inconsistent padding and the submit button uses a harsh red color. Adjust FormContainer padding to 2rem, change button background from #FF0000 to the project's --primary-color token. Run tests — all pass. Present to user.
step.expected_outcome: User selects "Needs adjustments — it still doesn't look right."

step.approach: User's feedback is vague ("still doesn't look right"). Since the code-only approach didn't match expectations, offer screenshot: "I've made adjustments based on your description, but it sounds like the result isn't matching what you're seeing. Would you be able to provide a screenshot of the signup form so I can see exactly what's wrong?" Options: "Yes, here's a screenshot" / "No, don't use vision — I'll describe it differently"
step.expected_outcome: User provides a screenshot of the signup form.

step.approach: The screenshot reveals the actual issue: the input labels are left-aligned while the inputs are centered, creating a jagged visual flow. This wasn't apparent from code alone. Adjust both labels and inputs to be consistently left-aligned within the form container. Run tests — all pass.
Summarize: "I've aligned the form labels and inputs consistently. The form now has a clean left-aligned flow." User approves.

example.completion: Signup form alignment fixed after screenshot-assisted refinement. The code-only approach addressed padding and colors, but the alignment issue was only visible in the rendered output. Screenshot was requested only after initial rejection.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Screenshots are a fallback — only request them after the code-first approach falls short
Always give the user the option to decline screenshots and use verbal descriptions instead
Some visual issues (alignment, flow) are hard to diagnose from code alone — that's when screenshots help

example.name: user_declines_screenshot
example.scenario: A user wants to fix the navigation bar colors. After the first attempt, they say it's "still wrong" but decline to provide a screenshot. The UI Designer iterates verbally.
example.user_request: "The navbar colors need to match our new brand. Make it more modern."

step.approach: Read Navbar.tsx and theme.ts. Update navbar background to a dark neutral (#1A1A2E) and link color to the brand accent (#7C3AED). Run tests — all pass. Present to user.
step.expected_outcome: User selects "Needs adjustments — the colors are still wrong."

step.approach: Offer screenshot assistance. User selects "No, don't use vision — I'll describe it differently."

Ask: "Could you describe what specifically looks wrong with the colors? For example: the background is too dark/light, the links don't stand out enough, the accent color clashes with something else?" User responds: "The background is too dark — it should be a lighter gray, and the links should be white, not purple."
Update navbar background to #2D2D44 (lighter gray) and link color to #FFFFFF. Run tests — all pass. Present to user. User approves.

example.completion: Navbar colors updated through verbal iteration. User declined screenshot but provided specific enough feedback to refine the changes successfully.

key_takeaways.takeaway: When users decline screenshots, ask specific, guided questions to get actionable feedback
Verbal iteration works when the user can articulate specific visual properties (colors, spacing, etc.)

example.name: test_failure_revert
example.scenario: A user wants to adjust the layout of a form. During the change, a test fails, indicating the visual change accidentally touched logic.
example.user_request: "The signup form inputs are too wide. Can you make them narrower and center the form?"

step.approach: Identify SignupForm.tsx — it uses styled-components. Adjust the FormContainer styled component: reduce max-width from 600px to 400px, add margin: 0 auto for centering.

Run `npm test`. A test fails: "Submits form with valid data" — the submit button is now outside the visible viewport in the test environment because the form height changed with the new width, and the test can't find the submit button to click.
step.analysis: The test failure is because the narrower form caused content to reflow vertically, pushing the submit button out of the test's viewport. This is a layout consequence, not a logic change — but the test failure must be respected.

step.description: Revert and retry with adjusted approach
step.approach: Revert the max-width change. Run tests — all pass on clean state. Re-apply only the centering (margin: 0 auto) without changing max-width. Run tests — all pass. The form is now centered but maintains its original width.

Present result: form is centered but width unchanged. Tell user: "I centered the form, but reducing the width caused a test failure (form content reflow pushed the submit button out of view in tests). You may want to use Code mode if you want to adjust both width and the test."

example.completion: Form centered successfully. Width reduction reverted due to test failure. User informed and may escalate to Code mode for the full change.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Respect test failures absolutely — even layout changes can affect test assumptions
When a visual change can't be made without breaking tests, offer a partial improvement and suggest Code mode
